children.text: Eightieth session – Agenda item 156 (a) – Financing of the United Nations peacekeeping forces in the Middle East: United Nations Disengagement Observer Force – Budget performance for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and budget for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 for the United Nations Disengagement Observer Force – Report of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions

table_content.text: Proposal submitted by the Secretary-General for 2026/27
Adjustment recommended by the Advisory Committee for 2026/27
Recommendation of the Advisory Committee for 2026/27

a	Estimates as at 28 February 2026.
	b	Excludes one-time requirements for termination entitlements payable to staff members.

a	Estimates as at 28 February 2026.
	b	Excludes one-time requirements for termination entitlements payable to staff members.

children.text: 1.	During its consideration of the report on the financing of the United Nations Disengagement Observer Force (UNDOF) (A/80/596 (advance version)), the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions met with representatives of the Secretary-General, who provided additional information and clarification, concluding with written responses received on 17 March 2026. The comments and recommendations of the Committee on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations are contained in its report (A/80/604), and those pertaining to the findings and recommendations of the Board of Auditors on the United Nations peacekeeping operations for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 can be found in its related report (A/80/633).

text: II.	Budget performance report for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025

children.text: 2.	By its resolution 79/307, the General Assembly appropriated an amount of $68,743,800 gross ($67,105,600 net) for the maintenance of the Force for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025. A total allotment was issued to the Force in the amount of $68,235,000 (or 99.3 per cent of the appropriation). Expenditure for the period totalled $67,872,200 gross ($65,895,100 net), with a budget implementation rate of 98.7 per cent, which reflects: (a) lower-than-budgeted expenditure under military and police personnel ($558,700, or 1.3 per cent), attributable primarily to the fewer items of contingent-owned major equipment; (b) higher-than-budgeted expenditure under civilian personnel ($976,000, or 6.3 per cent), attributable primarily to the higher actual requirements for international and national General Service staff; and (c) lower-than-budgeted expenditure under operational costs ($1,288,900, or 11.4 per cent), attributable to the lower actual costs of petrol, oil and lubricants and the reprioritization of resources in light of liquidity challenges.

3.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that redeployments in the amount of $976,000 to group II (civilian personnel) from group I (military and police personnel) and group III (operational costs) resulted from expenditures in excess of the appropriation for group II due to higher actual requirements for international and national General Service staff. The Advisory Committee trusts that more detailed information on redeployments will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of consideration of the present report and in the next report, in particular on activities curtailed and other lower-than-budgeted expenditures that allowed for resources to be redeployed and the criteria used by missions to reprioritize resources.

4.	The Advisory Committee notes from the information provided to it that, as at 30 June 2025, unliquidated obligations for 2024/25 amounted to $7,589,600 (or 11.0 per cent of the appropriation), representing an increase of $187,500 compared with the amount of $7,402,100 as at 30 June 2024 for the 2023/24 period. Upon enquiry, the Committee was informed that, as at 31 January 2026, unliquidated obligations had decreased to $915,000 (or 1.3 per cent of the total appropriation) for the 2024/25 period, comprising military and police personnel ($375,400), civilian personnel ($4,600) and operational costs ($535,000).

5.	The unencumbered balance, in gross terms, amounts to $871,600, or 1.3 per cent of the overall level of resources approved for the financial period. A detailed analysis of variances is provided in section III of the report of the Secretary-General on the budget performance of UNDOF for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025. Information provided to the Advisory Committee indicates that other revenue and adjustments for the 2024/25 period amount to $1,658,600, comprising: (a) investment revenue ($1,497,000); (b) other/miscellaneous revenue ($55,100); and (c) the cancellation of prior-period obligations ($106,500). The Committee notes that the report of the Secretary-General, in paragraphs 32 and 54, indicates that credits arising from the above-mentioned revenue and adjustments should be placed in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient, to enable the full, efficient and effective implementation of mandates (see para. 43 below).
Matters pertaining to the report of the Board of Auditors

6.	In considering the report of the Secretary-General on the financing of UNDOF, the Advisory Committee also had before it the report of the Board of Auditors on the accounts of the United Nations peacekeeping operations for the financial period ended 30 June 2025 (A/80/5 (Vol. II)). Regarding UNDOF, the Board noted issues in terms of the information provided on budget preparation and efficiency gains regarding transferred assets received from another peacekeeping mission, the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali. Upon enquiry, the Committee was informed that all recommendations from the 2022/23 and 2023/24 periods were fully implemented and the implementation of the recommendations issued in the 2024/25 period is in progress. The Advisory Committee trusts that the recommendations will be implemented in a timely manner.

text: III.	Information on performance for the current period

children.text: 7.	Regarding current and projected expenditure for the period from 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 28 February 2026, expenditure amounted to $34,122,300, or 49.0 per cent of the appropriation of $69,576,400. At the end of the current period, the estimated total expenditure would amount to $59,153,400, with a projected underexpenditure of $10,500,500, or 15 per cent, against the approved resources.

8.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as a result of the implementation of the contingency plan, the Force is currently not able to fully implement its mandate, and, while the Force has full visibility over the area of separation, its visibility over the area of limitation is partial. Furthermore, the Force was required to temporarily suspend operations at one of its military positions, United Nations position 22, which is currently inactive and is occasionally patrolled to ensure it is secure. The Force transferred its equipment to other positions and reinforced the position in order to protect it. Furthermore, the Charlie gate opens only on a temporary basis.

9.	The Advisory Committee was also informed that the liquidity crisis has significantly limited the Force’s procurement activities and overall budget implementation for the 2025/26 period. All non-urgent procurement activities have been postponed in order to prioritize the Force’s core mandated functions and maintain operational continuity. Further, as a result of personnel reductions under the current budget period, certain non-core activities, including quick-impact projects, have been temporarily suspended and maintenance activities have been carefully prioritized. In addition, patrolling in the outer areas of the area of limitation has been adjusted as a forced measure to align with the available personnel and resources. Although this adjustment reduces operational flexibility, it ensures that essential mandate-related tasks remain fully covered.

10.	The Advisory Committee notes that, while the contingency plan was formulated to ensure that essential mandate-related tasks remain fully covered, it nevertheless reduces operational flexibility, and the mission is currently not able to fully implement its mandate. The Committee trusts that all efforts will be undertaken to ensure the security and safety of personnel. The Committee also trusts that an update on and more detailed assessment of the impact of the contingency plan on the implementation of the mission’s mandate will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the report and in the next performance report.

11.	The Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 2 February 2026, a total of $2,500,073,000 had been assessed on Member States in respect of UNDOF since its inception. Payments received as at the same date amounted to $2,459,101,000, leaving an outstanding balance of $40,972,000. As at 27 January 2026, the cash available amounted to $40,500,200, which was sufficient to cover the three-month operating reserve of $10,125,700 (excluding reimbursements to troop- and police-contributing countries).

12.	The Advisory Committee was also informed that, as at 31 December 2025, the outstanding balance for contingent-owned equipment amounted to $9,180,800. The Committee was further informed that, as at 5 February 2026, $5,506,000 had been paid to settle 180 death and disability claims since the inception of the Force, and no death or disability compensation claims were pending. The Advisory Committee trusts that all outstanding liabilities and claims will be settled in a timely manner.

text: IV.	Proposed budget for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027

children.text: A.	Mandate and planning assumptions

13.	The mandate of UNDOF was established by the Security Council in its resolution 350 (1974) and was renewed most recently in its resolution 2811 (2025), by which it was extended until 30 June 2026. The planning assumptions and mission support initiatives of UNDOF for 2026/27 are summarized in section I.C of the report of the Secretary-General.
Operational constraints

14.	According to information provided to the Advisory Committee at the time of its consideration of the report, the situation in the region, particularly regarding Lebanon, was calmer, and UNDOF was not facing significant operational challenges. Remaining operational constraints were taken into account in planning for the 2026/27 period, with a focus on enhancing operational flexibility, mitigating supply chain risks and ensuring continuity of critical activities under restrictive operating conditions. The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information on the operational environment of the Force, and its implication on budgetary requirements, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in future budget reports.
Impact of the UN80 Initiative and culture of efficiency

15.	In his report, the Secretary-General indicates efficiency gains in section II.C. The cost estimates for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 take into account efficiency initiatives under military contingent personnel, civilian personnel, facilities and infrastructure, and ground transportation in the amount of $6,578,600. Estimated efficiencies for 2026/27 represent a 9.5 per cent reduction compared to the approved budget for 2025/26, in the context of an overall 11.4 per cent proposed reduction (see para. 18 below). Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the Force differentiates genuine efficiency gains from reduced activity levels by assessing whether savings stem from structural and process-based improvements, such as streamlined workflows, improved coordination, revised service delivery models and optimized asset utilization, or from revised outputs.

16.	The Advisory Committee was also informed that, while all core activities will continue, the measures to streamline organizational structures and align resources with core mandated activities, particularly within the military and civilian components, informed by the recalibration of the Force’s footprint following the implementation of the contingency plan in 2025/26, could result in reduced flexibility in responding to peak workloads or unforeseen operational demands. The Committee was informed that, to curb the potential challenges associated with reduced flexibility, the Force will improve internal planning and optimize operational modalities, improve workload prioritization and reallocation, and enhance oversight and coordination mechanisms between civilian and uniformed components to ensure continuity of mandated activities.

17.	As regards the reversibility of the contingency plan, the Advisory Committee was informed that this pertains to the Force’s ability to reactivate deferred capacities and projects in the context of the contingency plan should liquidity conditions improve. In comparison, the structural adjustments included in the proposed budget for 2026/27 reflect sustainable and non-reversible changes to optimize efficiency while safeguarding core mandate delivery. Furthermore, the reductions proposed in the budget for 2026/27 are part of a strategic approach to optimize the Force’s footprint and resource allocation. The Advisory Committee notes that, while some proposed reductions reflect structural efficiency gains resulting from process improvements, others are driven primarily by operational constraints, or by liquidity constraints and temporary expenditure compression. The Committee trusts that further information on the connection between the proposed reductions and changes in the expected accomplishments and outputs proposed for the mission for 2026/27 will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report, to allow for a better assessment of the sustainability and long-term implications of such measures on the implementation of the mission’s mandate. The Committee will further discuss efficiency measures in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
B.	Resource requirements

18.	Resource requirements of $61,615,100 for the period 2026/27 represent a decrease of $7,961,300, or 11.4 per cent, compared with the resources of $69,576,400 approved for the 2025/26 period. Information on variances between the apportionment for 2025/26 and the proposed resources for 2026/27 is contained in section III of the proposed budget.
Financial resources
(Thousands of United States dollars)

19.	Under the provisions of the status-of-forces agreement, estimated non-budgeted contributions for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 in terms of land provided by the host country amount to $1,035,000 (A/80/596, para. 28). The Advisory Committee acknowledges the continued non-budgeted contributions to UNDOF.
1.	Military and police personnel
a	Representing the highest level of authorized strength.
Delayed deployment factors
(Percentage of approved strength)

20.	The proposed resources for military and police personnel for 2026/27 amount to $36,680,100, reflecting a decrease of $6,442,200, or 14.9 per cent, compared with the apportionment of $43,122,300 for 2025/26. The decrease is attributable to: (a) the application of a higher vacancy rate of 19 per cent in the computation of the standard reimbursement and allowances, rations and contingent-owned equipment for military contingent personnel, and the mission subsistence allowance for military staff officers; and (b) lower costs anticipated for travel on rotation (commercial) based on actual expenditure patterns (ibid., para. 39).

21.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, for group I (military and police personnel), UNDOF reduced its Force reserve company and repatriated two out of the three platoons and the corresponding contingent-owned major equipment, with 220 military contingent personnel and 14 military staff officers repatriated for an estimated duration of six months. Of the 234 uniformed personnel repatriated as part of the contingency plan, 107 military contingent personnel and 8 military staff officers are expected to return to the Force in the 2026/27 period, in line with the Force’s minimum assessed requirement to maintain adequate operational flexibility, preserve monitoring continuity, including within the area of limitation (see also paras. 15–17 above), and ensure effective implementation of the mandate under prevailing conditions. The repatriation of 113 military contingent personnel and 6 military staff officers reflects permanent adjustments resulting from the recalibration of the Force’s footprint and the redirection of resources towards critical mandate implementation activities.

22.	The Advisory Committee notes that the Force expects 107 military contingent personnel and 8 military staff officers from among the 234 repatriated uniformed personnel, and the corresponding contingent-owned equipment of three armoured personnel carriers, to return to the area of operation in the 2026/27 period. The Committee also notes that, while the application of the delayed deployment factor would correspond to the deployment of 1,013 uniformed personnel, the return of 115 military personnel in addition to the 948 actually deployed personnel would correspond to the deployment of 1,063 military personnel. The Advisory Committee trusts that further clarification on planned deployment, including repatriated personnel expected to return to the area of operation, and the proposed delayed deployment factor will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.
23.	The Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for military and police personnel.
2.	Civilian personnel
a	Funded under general temporary assistance.

24.	The proposed resources for civilian personnel for the 2026/27 period amount to $15,322,700, reflecting a decrease of $521,900, or 3.3 per cent, compared with the apportionment of $15,844,600 for 2025/26, attributable primarily under: (a) national General Service staff, due to the non-requirement for overtime for driving services formerly provided by national General Service staff that will instead be provided by individual contractual personnel and to the proposed abolishment of four posts; and (b) general temporary assistance, due to the proposed abolishment of two international temporary positions, offset in part by the increase under international staff for the application of the higher common staff cost rate compared with the rate applied in 2025/2026 (A/80/596, paras. 41, 43 and 45).
Recommendations on posts and positions

25.	The Secretary-General proposes a total of 137 civilian staff for 2026/27, comprising 50 international staff and 87 national staff (1 National Professional Officer and 86 national General Service staff), and reflecting the following post changes: (a) abolishment of five posts: Legal Officer (P-4), Office of the Head of Mission/Force Commander, and three national General Service posts of Vehicle Technician, Transport Section, Electrician, Engineering Section, and Finance Assistant, Kuwait Joint Support Office; and (b) abolishment of two temporary positions: Military Liaison Officer (P-4), Office of the Head of Mission/Force Commander, and Engineering Officer (P-3), Engineering Section.

26.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the proposal is the result of a comprehensive functional review and analysis and that, based on the findings and the current operating environment, the proposed abolishment of these functions is not expected to have a negative impact on the Force’s ability to provide the required level of support to its substantive military component in implementing the mandate. The review also took into account the planning of remaining responsibilities and the prioritization and sequencing of operational activities to maintain core mandate delivery despite reduced specialized capacity. The Committee was provided with additional information on the results of the review and was informed that all posts and positions proposed for abolishment are vacant and that the abolishment of the functions would not impact the redistribution of tasks, the management of incident resolution, liaison with the parties or the upkeep of the operational infrastructure. The Committee was also informed that, following the closure of the Kuwait Joint Support Office, UNDOF will receive only education grant services and tier 2b support and client services as a non-core client of the Regional Service Centre in Entebbe, Uganda (RSCE) beginning 1 July 2026. The Committee makes further comments in this regard in its report on RSCE (A/80/604/Add.4).
Vacant posts

27.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 28 February 2026, 25 posts (1 P-5, 4 P-4, 2 P-3, 3 Field Service and 15 national General Service) were vacant, of which none had been vacant for over two years and seven were proposed for abolishment (2 P-4, 1 P-3 and 4 national General Service). The Committee was also informed that, as at 31 December 2025, one post was encumbered by a staff member with an approved request for the early separation programme. Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, for group II (civilian personnel), the Force adhered to a recruitment freeze on 17 of its vacant posts and positions, comprising 15 posts (8 international and 7 national) and 2 temporary international positions. The cumulative operational impact of the vacancies is significant and has placed considerable strain on the Force’s ability to meet standard support requirements for its substantive military operations. Taking into consideration the proposed abolishment of posts and positions, the remaining vacant posts as at 1 July 2025 are retained as reversible changes, consistent with the contingency plan currently under implementation, in order to ensure that any adjustments beyond the proposed abolishments allow for flexibility to be maintained while safeguarding core mandate delivery. The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information on the status of vacant posts will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next budget submission. The Committee also trusts that detailed and updated information on all staff of the mission approved for the early separation programme, as well as on related costs, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next report.
Vacancy rates
Vacancy rates
(Percentage)

28.	The Secretary-General indicates in his budget report the assumptions and approach taken to ensure that vacancy rates are based on, to the extent possible, the actual data while maintaining consistency across the proposed budgets. The vacancy factors take into account the high number of abolishments included in the proposed budget for 2026/27 (A/80/596, para. 35). The Advisory Committee trusts that the Secretary-General will provide updated information on the latest actual and actual average vacancy rates to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report, along with clear justification where the proposed rates differ from the actual rates, including as it relates to the impact of proposed abolishments and realistic projected recruitment and potential upcoming vacancies, to the extent possible.
29.	The Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for civilian personnel.
3.	Operational costs
(Thousands of United States dollars)

30.	The proposed requirements of $9,612,300 reflect a decrease of $997,200, or 9.4 per cent, for operational costs, mainly under facilities and infrastructure, ground transportation and communications and information technology. The decreases are offset mainly by increases under other supplies, services and equipment (ibid., sects. II and III).

31.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, for group III (operational costs), liquidity challenges resulted in the postponement of projects and deferred acquisitions for communications and information technology, infrastructure and operational requirements during the 2025/26 budget period. The proposed budget for 2026/27 was formulated based on the planning assumptions relevant to the period, taking into account operational necessity and the postponement.
Official travel

32.	The proposed resources amount to $297,500, reflecting a decrease of $900, or 0.3 per cent, compared with the apportionment of $298,400 for 2025/26. The Advisory Committee notes expenditures of $211,900 and $160,600 and apportioned amounts of $187,700 and $188,000 in the 2024/25 and 2023/24 periods, respectively. The Advisory Committee emphasizes that greater use of on-the-job training, virtual meetings, online platforms and online training tools should be made and reiterates its view that travel for conferences, workshops and professional meetings should be kept to a minimum in order to achieve efficiencies. Taking also into account the level of expenditure in previous years, the Committee recommends a reduction of 10 per cent ($29,800) to the proposed amount under official travel.
Facilities and infrastructure

33.	The proposed resources amount to $5,373,100, reflecting a decrease of $439,900, or 7.6 per cent, compared with the apportionment of $5,813,000 for 2025/26, attributable primarily to the lower estimated average price of fuel for generators of $0.747 per litre for 1.4 million litres, compared with $0.981 per litre for 1.6 million litres included in the approved budget (ibid., para. 49). The report of the Secretary-General indicates that UNDOF will continue routine restoration and major maintenance and repairs to preserve the functionality of roads, fences, snow poles, watchtowers, sentry points, water wells and other force infrastructure. Some of the work approved in earlier periods was postponed owing to the liquidity challenges and the presence of the Israel Defence Forces in the Force’s area of operations, and the work will resume in the 2026/27 period (ibid., para. 14). Supplementary information provided to the Advisory Committee on the major construction projects approved for 2024/25 and 2025/26 and planned for the 2026/27 period indicates that three projects are planned for the 2026/27 period in the total amount of $375,000, two in the Bravo side of the area of separation and one in the Alpha side. The Committee notes the information it received, upon enquiry, as to the current restrictions in the area of operations and the operational flexibility of the Force, as well as the ongoing liquidity challenges and the changing conditions in the region. The Advisory Committee is of the view that operational restrictions experienced by the Force may impact the implementation of the projects and therefore recommends a reduction of 5 per cent ($18,800) to the proposed amount of $375,000 for construction projects under facilities and infrastructure.
Petrol, oil and lubricants

34.	A total provision of $1,953,083 is proposed for fuel, oil and lubricants for the 2026/27 period, reflecting a decrease of $552,912, or 22.1 per cent, due mainly to changes in fuel costs and lower estimated required litres of fuel in the budget period. From the information provided to it, the Advisory Committee notes the efficiency measures of the Force and the reduced volume of fuel proposed under facilities and infrastructure and ground transportation. The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information on fuel costs will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report. The Committee discusses fuel costs and management in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
Medical

35.	The proposed resources amount to $289,700, reflecting no change compared with the apportionment for 2025/26. The Advisory Committee notes expenditures of $233,800 and $141,000 in the 2024/25 and 2023/24 periods, respectively, from the apportioned amounts of $299,900 and $306,700. The Committee also notes that, despite the reduced civilian and military staffing, the proposed level of resources for the 2026/27 period remains unchanged. The Advisory Committee, taking the past spending patterns into consideration, recommends a reduction of 5 per cent ($14,500) to the proposed amount under medical.
Quick-impact projects

36.	Information on quick-impact projects is provided in paragraphs 36 and 37 of the report of the Secretary-General, with proposed resources of $300,000, reflecting no change compared with 2025/26, for six projects. Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, for the 2024/25 period, all six projects were successfully completed and formally handed over to the relevant local authorities. In the context of the implementation of the contingency plan, the Force has suspended the six projects approved for the 2025/26 period, although it anticipates resuming activities in the 2026/27 period. The Committee was also informed that, at the end of the 2024/25 budget period, UNDOF was unable to conduct comprehensive year-end assessments of the projects due to the ongoing recruitment freeze, which impacted a post of Civil Affairs Officer. The Advisory Committee notes that assessments may be impacted in the 2026/27 period should recruitment restrictions continue. Nevertheless, the Committee is of the view that measures should be taken to ensure full oversight of project resources and that project results benefit the local population, as intended. The Committee trusts that updated information, including on planning, implementation and assessment, will be provided in the context of the next proposed budget report.
37.	Subject to the recommendations contained in paragraphs 32, 33 and 35 above, the Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for operational costs.
Environmental initiatives

38.	In his report, the Secretary-General indicates that, during the 2026/27 period, UNDOF will advance its environmental initiatives through the installation of solar power systems at one selected position. UNDOF will also continue the implementation of its environmental projects, including the installation of solar power systems that was postponed during the 2025/26 period, in order to enhance the self-sufficiency of all its positions while reducing its carbon footprint in line with the Organization’s environmental strategy (A/80/596, paras. 13 and 15). Regarding efficiency gains, an investment of $1,323,200 and cumulated efficiency gains of $570,000 have been recorded since inception, including the projections for the 2026/27 period. The Advisory Committee notes the continuing efforts of the Force to improve its environmental footprint and that efficiency gains have not yet offset investments. The Committee trusts that further efficiency gains will be generated from energy and environmental initiatives, and will be reflected in future reports. The Committee further discusses environmental activities and energy efficiency in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
Management of sea containers

39.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that UNDOF currently maintains a total of 329 containers, of which 310 are in active operational use, including 21 containers that are dedicated to security enhancement purposes. The remaining 19 containers are retained as operational reserve stock, and the Force has no damaged or destroyed containers. In 2024, the Force repurposed 100 containers for the construction of the centralized warehouse. As the units have since been structurally integrated, they now support a permanent facility. The Committee was also informed that, through periodic physical verifications and inventory management controls, the Force ensures that containers are utilized strictly for operational requirements. The Committee notes the initiatives taken by UNDOF regarding the management of containers and discusses the matter further in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
Equitable geographical representation and gender parity

40.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 31 December 2025, UNDOF comprised 122 staff members at all levels. Among the 43 international staff, 28 nationalities were represented. The Committee was also informed that, of the 122 staff members, 32 were women and 90 were men.

41.	As at 31 December 2025, international UNDOF staff were from the following regional groups: African States (8); Asia-Pacific States (16); Eastern European States (8); Latin American and Caribbean States (3); and Western European and other States (7).

42.	The Advisory Committee trusts that UNDOF will further pursue its efforts to promote equitable geographical representation and gender parity. The Committee further discusses equitable geographical representation and gender parity in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).

43.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly with regard to the financing of UNDOF for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 are indicated in section IV of the report of the Secretary-General (A/80/596). The Advisory Committee notes the unencumbered balance of $871,600 for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025, as well as other revenue amounting to $1,658,600 from investment revenue ($1,497,000), other/miscellaneous revenue ($55,100) and the cancellation of prior-period obligations ($106,500) for the period ended 30 June 2025. The Committee also notes the proposal of the Secretary-General to place the credits arising from the above-mentioned revenue and adjustments in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient, to enable the full, efficient and effective implementation of mandates. The Committee considers that this is a policy matter to be decided by the General Assembly. The Committee makes further comments in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).

44.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly with regard to the financing of UNDOF for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 are indicated in section IV of the report of the Secretary-General (A/80/596). Taking into account its observations and recommendations above, the Advisory Committee recommends that the proposed resources be reduced by $63,100, from $61,615,100 to $61,552,000. Accordingly, the Committee recommends that the General Assembly appropriate the amount of $61,552,000 for the maintenance of the Force for the 12-month period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027. A summary of the budgetary impact and the financial implications resulting from the recommendations of the Committee with regard to the financing of UNDOF for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 is presented in the table below.

Budgetary impact and financial implications resulting from the recommendations of the Advisory Committee for the United Nations Disengagement Observer Force for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027
